title: Judge worker /register has no rate limit or dedup — shared-token holder can flood judge_workers table
scenario: The register endpoint is gated only by the optional IP allowlist (allow-all when JUDGE_ALLOWED_IPS is unset, the default) plus the shared JUDGE_AUTH_TOKEN. Unlike claim/route.ts, it calls no consumeApiRateLimit/consumeUserApiRateLimit, and each successful POST unconditionally INSERTs a brand-new judge_workers row (db.insert(...).returning()) with no per-hostname dedup or cap. Anyone who obtains the shared judge token (a single secret shared across all workers and the registration path) — e.g. via a leaked CI/compose env, a backup, or a compromised worker host — can issue unbounded register calls and bloat the workers table, each row also being treated as 'online' and counted by the staleness sweep and admin inventory. This is a DoS/resource-exhaustion and audit-noise vector, not a verdict-forgery path.
suggestedFix: Add a rateLimit bucket keyed on client IP (and/or hostname) to the register handler, and/or upsert by (hostname, ipAddress) instead of always inserting a new row. Optionally cap the number of 'online'/non-deregistered workers per hostname and reject excess registrations.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against current code; every claimed mitigation is genuinely absent.

1. No rate limit on register. src/app/api/v1/judge/register/route.ts:24-32 gates the handler only with isJudgeIpAllowed (line 26) and isJudgeAuthorized (line 30). There is no consumeApiRateLimit/consumeUserApiRateLimit call. By contrast, claim/route.ts:122 explicitly calls consumeUserApiRateLimit("judge:claim"), confirming the asymmetry the finding describes.

2. Unconditional INSERT, no dedup. register/route.ts:49-64 runs db.insert(judgeWorkers).values({...status:"online"...}).returning() on every successful POST. The route exports only POST (no wrapper). isJudgeAuthorized (src/lib/judge/auth.ts:26) contains no throttling (the lone "rate" grep hit is the substring in "migRATEd").

3. Default gate is allow-all + shared token. src/lib/judge/ip-allowlist.ts:14-18,160-166: when JUDGE_ALLOWED_IPS is unset (the default), getAllowlist() returns null and isJudgeIpAllowed returns true for everyone. So the only real gate is the single shared JUDGE_AUTH_TOKEN — exactly the secret the threat model assumes leaks (CI/compose env, backup, compromised worker host).

4. No middleware-level throttle. The Next 16 middleware src/proxy.ts contains no rate limiting; furthermore proxy.ts:303-309 explicitly excludes judge worker routes (isJudgeWorkerRoute) from isProtectedRoute, so judge endpoints bypass middleware auth and rely solely on their route handlers. No src/middleware.ts exists.

5. No DB-level dedup/cap. src/lib/db/schema.pg.ts:416-449 defines judge_workers with NO unique constraint or index on hostname — only status and last_heartbeat indexes plus an active_tasks>=0 check. Nothing prevents unbounded duplicate-hostname rows.

Result: A holder of the shared judge token can issue unbounded register calls, each inserting a fresh status="online" judge_workers row counted by the staleness sweep (src/lib/judge/worker-staleness-sweep.ts) and admin inventory (src/app/api/v1/admin/workers/*). The DoS/resource-exhaustion and audit-noise vector is reachable and manifest right now. This is not a verdict-forgery path, consistent with the finding's own scoping.

Traced the full path from POST /api/v1/judge/register entry to the cited INSERT and confirmed the finding is genuinely present in current code.

Guards on the path (register/route.ts:26-43): (1) isJudgeIpAllowed — but ip-allowlist.ts:14-18,164 returns true (allow-all) when JUDGE_ALLOWED_IPS is unset, which the finding correctly notes is the default; (2) isJudgeAuthorized — auth.ts:26-35 only checks the SHARED JUDGE_AUTH_TOKEN via Bearer; (3) Zod schema validation. None of these is per-identity or stateful.

Crucially, register/route.ts has NO rate limiting: grep for consume*/rateLimit in the file returns nothing, and there is no Next.js middleware.ts anywhere in the repo (find returned empty), so no global limiter applies. By contrast, claim/route.ts:122 calls consumeUserApiRateLimit (a real exported limiter in src/lib/security/api-rate-limit.ts:195) with a per-worker/IP/auth-hash scope, confirming the asymmetry the finding describes.

The INSERT (register/route.ts:49-64) is unconditional: db.insert(judgeWorkers).values({...status:"online"...}).returning() with no onConflict, dedup-by-hostname, or per-token cap (grep for onConflict/dedup/cap/count/existing in the file returns nothing). Each successful POST creates a fresh row with status "online".

Impact confirmed: worker-staleness-sweep.ts only transitions rows online->stale->offline (lines 45-69) — it never DELETEs them, so flooded rows persist permanently in judge_workers (table bloat / audit noise) and are counted as online by admin inventory until the sweep timeout passes. This is a resource-exhaustion/audit-noise DoS vector, not verdict forgery, exactly as the finding scopes it.

The only precondition is possession of the shared JUDGE_AUTH_TOKEN, which the finding explicitly stipulates (leaked CI/compose env, backup, or compromised worker). Given any shared-token holder, the flood is reachable and unbounded in the current code. The hardening commits in this area (per-worker secret hashing) addressed verdict-forgery via claim/result, but did not add any rate limit or dedup/cap to register. isReal=true.

title: Cross-instructor IDOR: PATCH/DELETE /problems/[id] scopes READ to taught groups but lets any problems.edit/problems.delete holder mutate ANY problem org-wide
scenario: The built-in `instructor` role holds `problems.edit` and `problems.delete`. The read path `canAccessProblem` (src/lib/auth/permissions.ts:108-171) was hardened so that a non-admin `problems.view_all` holder can only read another group's PRIVATE problem if it is linked to a group they actually teach (lines 140-155), and `getManageableProblemsForGroup` / `getAccessibleProblemIds` similarly prevent pulling another group's private problem into an assignment (management.ts:133-174, quick-create/route.ts:55-81). But the WRITE path here applies NO group/ownership/visibility scoping: it only checks `problems.edit` (or authorship). So Instructor A (teaching Class A) can `PATCH /api/v1/problems/{privateProblemOfClassB}` to rewrite the statement, flip visibility, or — critically — replace the test cases of Class B's private/exam problem they have no group relationship to (updateProblemWithTestCases takes no scope arg, problem-management.ts:287). On a problem with no submissions yet (e.g. an upcoming exam) the test-case lock at line 100 does not trigger, so the test data can be silently altered, destroying exam/contest integrity, or the problem can be DELETEd (line 196/222) if it has no submissions/links. This is a genuine read/write authorization asymmetry, not the intended org-wide-admin model (only `groups.view_all` admins are meant to be org-wide).
suggestedFix: Scope the write authorization the same way `canAccessProblem` scopes reads: allow edit/delete only when the actor is the author, holds `groups.view_all` (true org-wide admin), or the problem is linked (problemGroupAccess) to a group the actor teaches (getAssignedTeachingGroupIds). Add a `canManageProblem(problemId, userId, role)` helper mirroring canAccessProblem's group-scoped branch and call it in both PATCH and DELETE before mutating.
verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The cross-instructor IDOR on write is genuinely present in current code.

Asymmetry confirmed:
- READ paths scope a non-admin problems.view_all holder to taught groups: GET (src/app/api/v1/problems/[id]/route.ts:53), export (export/route.ts:35), draft (draft/route.ts:32/52/71) all call canAccessProblem, which restricts a non-groups.view_all user to problems linked to groups they teach (src/lib/auth/permissions.ts:140-155). The list route (problems/route.ts:68-99) applies the same getAssignedTeachingGroupIds filter. This read scoping was deliberately added 2026-05-31 ("fix(rbac): scope problems.view_all access to taught groups").
- WRITE paths do NOT scope. PATCH gate (route.ts:88 `if (!isAuthor && !canEditProblem) return forbidden();`) and DELETE gate (route.ts:196) check ONLY the global capability problems.edit / problems.delete or authorship. Grep confirms PATCH/DELETE invoke neither canAccessProblem, nor getAssignedTeachingGroupIds, nor any problemGroupAccess check — the only such references in [id]/route.ts are the import (line 9) and the GET handler (line 53).

Capability premise confirmed: built-in instructor role HAS problems.edit and problems.delete (src/lib/capabilities/defaults.ts:40-41) but does NOT have groups.view_all (only admin/super_admin, defaults.ts:90). So an instructor teaching Class A passes both write gates for ANY problem org-wide, including Class B's private problem they have no group relationship to.

Handler wrapper provides no compensating control: PATCH/DELETE configs (route.ts:76-78, 186-187) pass only rateLimit/schema, so createApiHandler applies only default auth:true (any authenticated user) with no capabilities/roles restriction (src/lib/api/handler.ts:120-136); the per-request gate is entirely the unscoped capability check in the handler body.

Exploitable impact confirmed:
- Statement/metadata/visibility rewrite: updateProblemWithTestCases (src/lib/problem-management.ts:287-321) updates problems by problemId with no scope/ownership arg, setting title/description/visibility/limits unconditionally. Visibility flip is not even gated by problems.manage_visibility in PATCH.
- Test-case tampering on an upcoming exam: the lock at route.ts:100 only triggers when hasExistingSubmissions is true; a problem with no submissions yet (e.g. upcoming exam) has hasExistingSubmissions=false, so body.testCases are silently applied via syncProblemTestCases (problem-management.ts:313) with no scope.
- DELETE (route.ts:196-243) likewise succeeds for a problem with no submissions/assignment links (or with force + problems.delete).

No mitigation found and the path is reachable today. This is a real read/write authz asymmetry, not the intended org-wide-admin (groups.view_all) model.

VERIFIED REAL. End-to-end trace confirms a genuine read/write authorization asymmetry.

Entry point: createApiHandler (src/lib/api/handler.ts:92-210) only enforces auth + optional role/capability + CSRF + Zod. PATCH/DELETE for /problems/[id] pass NO roles/capabilities config, so the wrapper adds no per-resource scoping. No Next.js middleware.ts exists anywhere in the repo, so authz is purely handler-level.

PATCH (src/app/api/v1/problems/[id]/route.ts:79-152): loads the target problem by id with no group/ownership filter (line 81); the sole authz gate is line 88 `if (!isAuthor && !canEditProblem) return forbidden();` where canEditProblem = caps.has("problems.edit"). It never calls canAccessProblem or any group-scope helper (canAccessProblem is imported only for GET at line 53). DELETE (lines 188-223) mirrors this with line 196 `if (!isAuthor && !canDeleteProblem) return forbidden();`.

Read path contrast confirmed: canAccessProblem (src/lib/auth/permissions.ts:108-171) scopes a non-admin problems.view_all holder to groups they actually teach (lines 140-155). This hardening is entirely bypassed on the write path.

Role reality confirmed: built-in instructor role (src/lib/capabilities/defaults.ts:36-80) holds problems.edit, problems.delete, problems.view_all but NOT groups.view_all (groups.view_all is added only at the admin tier, defaults.ts:90). So an instructor is exactly the non-org-admin actor the read hardening targets — yet the write path treats any problems.edit/delete holder as org-wide.

Mutation has no scope arg: updateProblemWithTestCases (src/lib/problem-management.ts:287) takes only (problemId, input, actorId) and unconditionally rewrites the problem row, test cases, tags, and file links for the given id.

Test-case-lock bypass confirmed: the lock at route.ts:100 fires only when hasExistingSubmissions is true. For an upcoming exam/contest problem with zero submissions, the lock does not trigger, so Instructor A (teaching Class A) can PATCH Class B's private exam problem to rewrite the statement, flip visibility, or silently replace test cases — and DELETE it (route.ts:196/222) if it has no submissions/assignment links. This is a reachable, exploitable cross-instructor IDOR on the mutate path, distinct from the intended groups.view_all org-wide-admin model.

title: Within-group ownership escalation: co-instructor can transfer group ownership (instructorId) via PATCH /groups/[id]
scenario: PATCH /groups/[id] authorizes via `canManageGroupResourcesAsync(group.instructorId, user.id, user.role, id)` (management.ts:72-86), which returns true for a `group_instructors.role = 'co_instructor'`. The body schema (validators/groups.ts:18) permits `instructorId`, and the handler writes it directly (lines 142-155) with the only constraint being that the new instructor is active and has role level > 0. A co-instructor can therefore reassign the owning `instructorId` to themselves or any other user, removing the original owner's ownership (the original owner then loses the `group.instructorId === userId` fast-path in every canManageGroupResourcesAsync/canAccessGroup check). This is an ownership takeover by a non-owner that the owner did not authorize, affecting grade overrides, assignment edits, exam proctoring scope, and member management for the whole class.
suggestedFix: Gate the `instructorId` (ownership transfer) field separately from general group edits: require that the actor is the current owner OR holds an org-wide capability (e.g. `groups.view_all`/admin), not merely co-instructor. Reject `instructorId` changes in the PATCH handler when `group.instructorId !== user.id` and the actor lacks `groups.view_all`.
verifierReasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. The PATCH /groups/[id] handler at src/app/api/v1/groups/[id]/route.ts gates the entire body (including instructorId reassignment) behind a single shared authorization check at lines 127-134: `canManage = canManageGroupResourcesAsync(group.instructorId, user.id, user.role, id)` (OR a global groups.edit capability). There is NO owner-only guard before the instructorId write.

canManageGroupResourcesAsync (src/lib/assignments/management.ts:72-86) returns true for a group_instructors row with role 'co_instructor' (line 81-84 via getGroupInstructorAssignmentRole). This is asserted directly by the test at tests/unit/assignments/management.test.ts:106-110 (`canManageGroupResourcesAsync('owner-1','co-1',...)` resolves true with a co_instructor row). Co-instructors are reachable: they are added via POST /groups/[id]/instructors with role enum ['co_instructor','ta'] (instructors/route.ts:12), itself gated only by the same canManage check.

The instructorId write (route.ts:142-155) applies only one constraint on the TARGET: nextInstructor must exist, be active, and have getRoleLevel(role) > 0. It does NOT verify the actor is the current owner, nor block demoting the existing owner. The schema permits instructorId (src/lib/validators/groups.ts:18-21) and createApiHandler (src/lib/api/handler.ts) does not strip it.

Impact is genuine: canAccessGroup uses an owner fast-path `if (group.instructorId === userId)` (src/lib/auth/permissions.ts:42) and canManageGroupResources baseline is `groupInstructorId === userId` (management.ts:31). Reassigning instructorId away from the original owner strips that fast-path from the original owner across all group permission checks.

No mitigation exists. The introducing commit 8315bf9f explicitly lists 'Not-tested: Live instructor reassignment flow with existing co-instructors/TAs', and the only tests (tests/unit/api/group-edit-implementation.test.ts) merely assert the field is writable — none assert a co-instructor is blocked. A co-instructor can therefore take over group ownership (or hand it to a third user), a non-owner-authorized escalation affecting grade overrides, assignment edits, exam scope, and member management. isReal=true.

Traced end-to-end against current code; the privilege-escalation path is genuinely reachable and unmitigated.

ENTRY POINT: PATCH /groups/[id] is wrapped by createApiHandler with only { rateLimit, schema } — NO auth.roles/auth.capabilities restriction (route.ts:115-118). handler.ts only enforces "any authenticated user" + CSRF + schema validation (handler.ts:120-173). All authorization is delegated to the in-handler check.

AUTHORIZATION GATE (route.ts:127-134): canManage = canManageGroupResourcesAsync(group.instructorId, user.id, user.role, id). In management.ts:72-86 this returns true when getGroupInstructorAssignmentRole(groupId, userId) === "co_instructor" (management.ts:81-84). A co-instructor therefore passes the gate (`if (!canManage && !canEditByCapability) return forbidden()` does not fire).

REACHABLE ACTOR: A co_instructor is a real, assignable role. POST /groups/[id]/instructors accepts role enum ["co_instructor","ta"] (instructors/route.ts:10-13, 96-100), and schema.pg.ts:238 confirms the group_instructors.role column.

VULNERABLE WRITE (route.ts:136-157): updateGroupSchema permits instructorId (validators/groups.ts:16-22). The instructorId branch (route.ts:142-155) applies NO owner-only / org-admin guard — the only constraints are that the target exists, is active, and getRoleLevel(role) > 0. It then writes updates.instructorId = instructorId unconditionally and persists it (route.ts:154,157). There is no check that user.id === group.instructorId or that the caller holds groups.view_all for the instructorId field specifically.

IMPACT CONFIRMED: canManageGroupResources (management.ts:27-32) and canAccessGroup (permissions.ts:42) both rely on the group.instructorId === userId owner fast-path. After a co-instructor PATCHes instructorId to themselves/another user, the original owner loses that fast-path across resource-management and access checks (unless they separately hold a global capability or a group_instructors row).

No upstream auth/scope/validation guard blocks this. A co_instructor can take over group ownership, which the original owner never authorized. Finding is real and currently exploitable.

title: GET /groups/[id]/assignments/[assignmentId]/exam-session allows reading another enrolled user's exam timing with only contests.view_analytics + group access (no group-manage relationship)
line: 97-130 (GET: targetUserId derivation at 120, access gate at 98)
scenario: In the GET handler the only group gate is `canAccessGroup` (line 98), which passes for any enrolled user. The `?userId=` override is honored when the actor `canManage` OR merely holds the global `contests.view_analytics` capability (line 120), with no requirement that the actor actually manages/teaches THIS group. A user who is enrolled in the group (e.g., as a participant) and whose role carries `contests.view_analytics` could read another enrolled participant's exam `startedAt`/`personalDeadline` for a windowed exam. Unlike the proctoring surfaces (canMonitorContest scopes anti_cheat.view_events to assigned groups), this capability is not scoped to the actor's taught groups, so it is a small cross-participant timing-info leak rather than a self-only view.
suggestedFix: Require a group-manage relationship for the `?userId=` override instead of a bare global capability: use `canManageContest`/`canMonitorContest` (which scope contests.view_analytics-style capabilities to the actor's assigned groups) before honoring an arbitrary target userId, mirroring participant-timeline/anti-cheat routes.
verifierReasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The cited code at src/app/api/v1/groups/[id]/assignments/[assignmentId]/exam-session/route.ts:93-138 matches the finding exactly.

Access flow in the GET handler:
- Line 98 gate is `canAccessGroup`, which (permissions.ts:55-59) passes for any enrolled participant.
- Line 120: `targetUserId = (canManage || caps.has("contests.view_analytics")) ? (?userId ?? self) : self`. `caps` comes from `resolveCapabilities(user.role)` (cache.ts), a purely role-based, NOT group-scoped, capability set.
- Lines 124-129 only verify the TARGET is enrolled in the group; they do not require the ACTOR to manage/teach this group.
- `canManageGroupResourcesAsync` (management.ts:72-86) is only true for the group owner, a `groups.view_all` holder, or a `co_instructor` row. So an actor who is merely enrolled, with no instructor row, has `canManage=false` but can still set `?userId=` purely on the strength of the global `contests.view_analytics` capability, then read another participant's `startedAt`/`personalDeadline` (lines 132-136).

Reachability is concrete:
- In the default role model, `instructor` carries `contests.view_analytics` (defaults.ts:66) but NOT `groups.view_all`. An instructor enrolled as a participant in a group owned by someone else (no group_instructors row) yields canManage=false yet caps.has("contests.view_analytics")=true — exactly the finding's scenario, leaking a co-participant's exam timing.
- Custom roles make it even cleaner: the capability catalog (types.ts:142-151) exposes `contests.view_analytics` as a standalone toggle independent of `submissions.view_all`/`groups.view_all`, so an admin-configured role can hold it alone.

Crucially, the sibling routes that expose the SAME analytics under the SAME capability DO apply a second group-scoping gate that this route lacks: contests/[assignmentId]/participant-timeline/[userId]/route.ts:12-15 and code-snapshots/[userId]/route.ts:14-17 both call `canViewAssignmentSubmissions(...)` after the capability check and 403 on failure; that function (submissions.ts:372-401) ultimately requires `hasGroupInstructorRole` for the specific group (absent org-wide `submissions.view_all`). The exam-session GET route has no equivalent second gate — no `canViewAssignmentSubmissions`, no teaching-group scoping — so it is strictly broader and the claimed cross-participant timing-info leak is present and exploitable right now. No existing mitigation prevents the scenario.

CONFIRMED REAL. End-to-end trace of GET /groups/[id]/assignments/[assignmentId]/exam-session (route.ts:93-138):

- Wrapper auth: the GET has no `auth:` config in createApiHandler, so it only requires an authenticated user (handler.ts:120-136) — no role/capability gate upstream.
- canAccessGroup (permissions.ts:55-59) returns true for ANY user holding an enrollments row, so a mere participant passes the line-98 gate.
- canManageGroupResourcesAsync (management.ts:72-86) is true only for group owner, groups.view_all (admin+), or co_instructor — FALSE for a non-managing enrolled user.
- Line 120: targetUserId honors attacker-controlled ?userId= when `canManage || caps.has("contests.view_analytics")`. The analytics capability path has NO requirement that the actor manage/teach THIS group.
- Lines 124-129 only verify the target is enrolled, not that the actor manages the group; getExamSession (exam-sessions.ts:122-142) returns startedAt/personalDeadline, returned at lines 133-136.

Capability reachability: contests.view_analytics is granted to built-in instructor (level 2) and admin+ by default (defaults.ts:66) and can be on custom roles. It is NOT admin-gated (groups.view_all is admin-only). The enrollments table (schema.pg.ts:203-224) has no role column, so an instructor-role (or custom-role) user can be enrolled as a plain participant in a different instructor's windowed-exam group. There, canManage is false but caps.has(contests.view_analytics) is true, so ?userId=<other enrolled participant> reads that participant's exam timing.

Asymmetry confirms it is an unintended outlier: the sibling participant-timeline route gates the SAME capability AND additionally calls canViewAssignmentSubmissions (route.ts:12), which (submissions.ts:386-400) requires submissions.view_all OR (assignments.view_status AND hasGroupInstructorRole of THIS group). The anti-cheat surface scopes anti_cheat.view_events to assigned groups via canMonitorContest (contests.ts:235-249). The exam-session GET omits any such taught-group scoping on the analytics path. No upstream guard blocks the path. Result: a small cross-participant timing-info leak (startedAt/personalDeadline) for windowed exams, exactly as described.

title: IOI partial scores are inflated: worker stops at first failing test, but server computes score as passed / results.length (truncated denominator)
scenario: The Rust worker runs test cases sequentially and breaks out of the loop the moment a verdict is not Accepted (executor.rs:617 `if verdict != Verdict::Accepted { break; }`). It then POSTs only the results it actually ran to /api/v1/judge/poll. The server is the sole place the numeric score is computed: computeFinalJudgeMetrics (verdict.ts:46) does `score = Math.round((passed * 10000) / results.length) / 100`, where results.length is the TRUNCATED count, not the total number of test cases. Example: a problem with 10 test cases where the submission passes tests 1-2 and fails test 3. The worker sends only 3 results (2 accepted, 1 failing). The server computes 2/3 = 66.67% instead of the true 2/10 = 20%. This percentage feeds every IOI scoring surface: mapSubmissionPercentageToAssignmentPoints and buildIoiLatePenaltyCaseExpr both do score/100 * points, so the leaderboard (contest-scoring.ts), gradebook (submissions.ts getAssignmentStatusRows), live rank, and CSV/JSON exports all over-credit. The unit test tests/unit/judge/verdict.test.ts ('2 out of 4 accepted' -> 50) hand-builds a COMPLETE 4-element array including the two trailing failures, which masks the bug because the real worker never sends trailing-failure rows. ICPC is unaffected (AC requires score==100, which truncation never produces on a failed run), but every IOI assignment/contest/recruiting test using partial credit is mis-scored, and the inflation magnitude grows with how early the failure occurs and how many total tests exist.
suggestedFix: Make the worker and server agree on the denominator. Either: (a) remove the early break so the worker runs and reports ALL test cases (simplest, restores the contract the server assumes; accept the extra runtime), or (b) have the worker continue running but if early-exit is desired for performance, push a synthetic 'skipped'/non-accepted result row for every remaining test case so results.length equals the true total, or (c) include the total test-case count in the report payload and have computeFinalJudgeMetrics divide passed by the true total (the server already has the test cases via the claim and can recompute the denominator from submission's problem). Add an integration test that drives the real worker output shape (truncated list) through the poll route and asserts the stored score uses the full denominator.
verifierReasoning:
I tried to refute this finding but found no mitigation at any layer; the bug is genuinely present and manifest.

End-to-end trace:
1. Worker (judge-worker-rs/src/executor.rs:515-619): iterates submission.test_cases, pushes one TestResult per executed case, and UNCONDITIONALLY breaks at line 617-619 on the first non-Accepted verdict. The Submission struct (types.rs:212-240) carries NO judging-mode / IOI / scoring-mode field, so the worker is mode-agnostic and short-circuits identically for all problem types. The only report carrying results is the final report_result at line 629, which passes the TRUNCATED results vec. The in-progress "judging" report (line 270-281) sends no results at all. git blame confirms line 617-619 is current live code (committed 2026-03, no later override).

2. Validator (src/lib/validators/api.ts:27-33): judgeStatusReportSchema declares results as z.array(...).optional() with NO minimum length, NO total-test-count field, and NO completeness check.

3. Server poll route (src/app/api/v1/judge/poll/route.ts:134): calls computeFinalJudgeMetrics(results) directly on the truncated array. There is no comparison against the submission's true testCases.length and no padding/reconciliation of missing trailing failures.

4. Score formula (src/lib/judge/verdict.ts:46): score = Math.round((passed * 10000) / results.length) / 100 — denominator is the truncated results.length, NOT total test cases. For a 10-test problem passing tests 1-2 and failing test 3, the worker sends 3 results -> 2/3 = 66.67% instead of the true 2/10 = 20%.

5. IOI scoring uses the stored score directly: buildIoiLatePenaltyCaseExpr (src/lib/assignments/scoring.ts:138-165) and mapSubmissionPercentageToAssignmentPoints compute LEAST(GREATEST(score,0),100)/100 * points. The leaderboard query (src/lib/assignments/contest-scoring.ts:234) uses buildIoiLatePenaltyCaseExpr("score","points") off the stored submissions.score column — it does NOT recompute from submission_results joined against the full test-case set. So the inflated score flows to leaderboard/gradebook/live-rank/exports.

The masking unit test "2 out of 4 accepted -> 50" (tests/unit/judge/verdict.test.ts:73-81) hand-builds a COMPLETE 4-element array including the two trailing failures, which the real worker never emits — confirming the finding's claim that the test hides the bug.

ICPC is unaffected because AC requires ROUND(score,2)=100 (contest-scoring.ts:236), which a truncated denominator never produces on a failed run. But IOI partial-credit scoring is inflated, with magnitude growing the earlier the failure and the larger the total test set.

CONFIRMED REAL. Traced the full path end-to-end; no upstream guard mitigates it.

1. Worker breaks early (judge-worker-rs/src/executor.rs:617-619): inside the `for test_case in &submission.test_cases` loop (line 515), there is an unconditional `if verdict != Verdict::Accepted { break; }`. The worker therefore stops at the first non-AC test and only pushes results for the tests it actually ran. The `Submission` struct (types.rs:211-239) has NO scoring-mode/run-all field, so the worker has zero IOI awareness — it always fails-fast regardless of assignment scoring model. `report_result` sends only the truncated `results` vec; no total-test-case count is transmitted (validators/api.ts judgeStatusReportSchema only carries `results: z.array(...).optional()`, no total).

2. Server uses the truncated denominator (src/lib/judge/verdict.ts:46): `score = Math.round((passed * 10000) / results.length) / 100`. The poll route (src/app/api/v1/judge/poll/route.ts:134) passes the worker's truncated `results` array directly to computeFinalJudgeMetrics, then stores `score` (line 147). There is no padding to the problem's true test-case count and no reconciliation — grep confirms verdict.ts:46 is the ONLY place score is computed in the judge flow. Concrete example holds: 10 total tests, pass tests 1-2, fail test 3 → worker sends 3 results (2 AC, 1 fail) → server stores 2/3 = 66.67% instead of true 2/10 = 20%.

3. Inflated percentage propagates to every IOI surface. buildIoiLatePenaltyCaseExpr (scoring.ts:149-165) and mapSubmissionPercentageToAssignmentPoints (scoring.ts:13-59) both do `LEAST(GREATEST(score,0),100)/100 * points`, reading the stored percentage as-is. These feed contest-scoring.ts:234 (leaderboard bestScore), leaderboard.ts:224 (live rank), submissions.ts:642 (gradebook), participant-timeline.ts, contest-analytics.ts, and recruiting-results.ts — all over-credit.

4. ICPC unaffected, as claimed: contest-scoring.ts:236 keys AC off `ROUND(score::numeric,2) = 100`; a run that hit the break always contains a non-AC row so passed < results.length, never yielding 100.

5. The masking unit test is real (tests/unit/judge/verdict.test.ts:73-82): the "2 out of 4 accepted" case hand-builds a COMPLETE 4-element array including trailing tc3/tc4 `wrong_answer` rows that the live worker would never emit (it breaks after tc3). So the suite asserts 50 against an array shape that the real fail-fast worker never produces, hiding the truncation.

Every link in the chain (early break -> truncated POST -> passed/results.length -> stored score -> IOI points multiplication) is present in current code with no mitigating guard. Reachable on any normal IOI partial-credit submission that fails before the last test.

title: Assignment PATCH does not invalidate the leaderboard ranking cache, so latePenalty/deadline/points/scoringModel edits leave stale standings and diverge from the gradebook

title: Frozen-window self live rank ignores score overrides while the frozen board applies them, so a student's displayed own-rank can contradict the standings
line: 208-259 (computeSingleUserLiveRank IOI/ICPC paths have no score_overrides overlay)
scenario: computeContestRanking overlays score_overrides for IOI (contest-scoring.ts:289-297,353-410) so the authoritative board reflects instructor-adjusted scores. During the freeze window the leaderboard route shows students the frozen board PLUS their own live rank from computeSingleUserLiveRank (leaderboard.ts:108). That function explicitly does NOT overlay score_overrides (acknowledged in the comment at leaderboard.ts:209-215 as deferred N7-C7), and ICPC live rank never overlays either. If an instructor overrides a student's problem score during a frozen contest, the student's own 'live rank' badge is computed from the raw judged score, which can place them above/below their true override-adjusted position — an inconsistency the same student can observe between their badge and the rest of the (override-aware) board once unfrozen, and between this surface and the gradebook.
suggestedFix: Overlay score_overrides in computeSingleUserLiveRank for IOI using the existing per-problem-best CTE (replace the per-problem best with the override when present, matching contest-scoring.ts semantics), or stop showing a live-rank badge when any score override exists for the assignment. For ICPC, make a product decision but at minimum document/suppress the badge when overrides are present so the displayed rank never contradicts the authoritative standings.
verifierReasoning:
Verified against current code; I could not find a mitigation, and the code itself confirms the gap rather than refuting it.

- computeContestRanking loads score_overrides UNCONDITIONALLY (no freeze cutoff) and overlays them for IOI (contest-scoring.ts:289-297, 353-410). So the frozen board a student sees — including the student's own row rank (entry.rank) — is override-adjusted.
- computeSingleUserLiveRank does NOT overlay score_overrides in either the ICPC path (leaderboard.ts:135-193) or the IOI path (leaderboard.ts:217-259). It computes purely from submissions.score. The comment at leaderboard.ts:209-215 explicitly states the override overlay here "remains a deferred sub-item of N7-C7" — i.e. acknowledged-but-open, not mitigated.
- The live-rank badge is produced and shown to the student ONLY during the freeze window (route.ts:58-61: !isInstructorView && leaderboard.frozen), attached to the student's own row (route.ts:79) and rendered as a "Live #{rank}" / "실시간 {rank}위" badge (leaderboard-table.tsx:404-410; messages/ko.json:2160, messages/en.json:2160).
- Overrides can be written at any time, including during the freeze window; there is no guard blocking them, and the override route invalidates the ranking cache (overrides/route.ts:128), so the frozen board immediately reflects the override.
- The gradebook (submissions.ts:674-728) also overlays overrides, confirming the third divergent surface.

No guard, fallback, or cache behavior prevents this; the cited comment confirms the deferral instead of closing it.

Severity is low and partially mitigated by UX framing: the badge is explicitly labeled "Live" (an indicative real-time estimate intended to differ from the frozen snapshot rank), and the student's actual board ROW rank is override-aware; only the secondary live-estimate badge omits overrides. But the factual inconsistency (override-ignoring live rank vs override-aware standings/gradebook) is genuinely present and manifest in the current code, so the finding is real, not refuted.

title: Configured 90-day audit-event retention is never enforced (audit_events grows forever, retaining candidate PII)
scenario: DATA_RETENTION_DAYS.auditEvents = 90 is defined (src/lib/data-retention.ts:2,18) and instrumentation.ts:24 calls startAuditEventPruning() at boot, but that function is an explicit no-op whose comment claims 'pruning is handled by startSensitiveDataPruning()'. This matters for data-isolation/GDPR because audit rows hold candidate PII directly: e.g. recruiting_invitation.created stores resourceLabel = candidateName and details.candidateEmail (recruiting-invitations/route.ts:114-116), and account_password_reset / revoked / deleted events store candidateName in resourceLabel. The documented retention promise (and any privacy-policy claim derived from it) is silently broken, and candidate names/emails outlive every other retention window forever.

There is NO auditEvents prune and no pruneAuditEvents function in that file. No other in-process timer deletes audit_events.

That endpoint is DISABLED BY DEFAULT: route.ts:15-25 returns HTTP 410 unless ENABLE_CRON_CLEANUP=true/1. There is no pg_cron, partition drop, or DB-side TTL on audit_events.

The docstrings in cleanup.ts:14-20 and route.ts:10-21/47-50 falsely assert "in-process pruners handle all data retention cleanup automatically" and reference a nonexistent pruneOldAuditEvents in audit/events.ts; the in-process path provably never deletes audit_events.

title: Permanent account deletion does not erase a recruiting candidate's PII held on the recruiting_invitations row (GDPR right-to-erasure gap)
scenario: When an admin permanently deletes a candidate (DELETE ?permanent=true), the handler runs only db.delete(users). The recruiting_invitations FK on userId is onDelete:'set null' (schema.pg.ts:971), so the invitation row survives with candidateName (NOT NULL), candidateEmail, ipAddress, and metadata (which can hold recruiter-supplied custom PII fields) fully intact — only the user link is nulled. The candidate's name/email were copied onto the users row at redeem (recruiting-invitations.ts:662-671), so erasing the user does NOT erase the source-of-record copy on the invitation. Worse, there is no admin path to delete a redeemed invitation at all: the invitation DELETE route rejects any non-pending invitation (recruiting-invitations/[invitationId]/route.ts:186-188). The only removal mechanism is the unrelated time-based pruneRecruitingInvitations sweep keyed on updatedAt, which a recruiter can indefinitely postpone by re-touching the row. A GDPR/erasure request fulfilled via 'permanently delete user' therefore leaves the candidate's PII in the database.
suggestedFix: In the permanent-delete branch, within the same transaction also scrub the candidate's invitation PII: either delete recruiting_invitations rows for that userId, or null/replace candidateName, candidateEmail, ipAddress and strip non-_sys metadata while keeping the audit shell. Also relax the invitation DELETE route (or add an erase endpoint) so a redeemed candidate's invitation PII can be removed on request, and record the erasure as an audit event.

I was asked to refute this finding but, after reading the cited code and all callers/guards/migrations, no mitigation exists and the gap is real and manifest.

VERIFIED FACTS:
1. Both permanent-delete paths run ONLY db.delete(users) with NO invitation cleanup:
   - API: src/app/api/v1/users/[id]/route.ts:473 (db.delete(users).where(eq(users.id, id))). Surrounding handler (442-475) only validates confirmUsername and records audit; never touches recruitingInvitations.
   - Server action: src/lib/actions/user-management.ts:212 (db.delete(users)). grep confirms the file has zero references to recruitingInvitations/contestAccessTokens cleanup; lines 205-230 show only audit recording around the delete.

2. The FK is ON DELETE set null at both ORM and DB level:
   - schema.pg.ts:970-971 userId references users.id onDelete:"set null".
   - drizzle/pg/0006_secret_abomination.sql:20 confirms the actual SQL constraint: recruiting_invitations_user_id_users_id_fk ... ON DELETE set null. So the invitation row SURVIVES, only userId is nulled.

3. PII is genuinely on the invitation row and survives: candidateName text NOT NULL (schema.pg.ts:966), candidateEmail (967), ipAddress (974), metadata jsonb (968). The redeem flow (recruiting-invitations.ts:662-671) copies candidateName/candidateEmail onto the users row and (690-697) writes ipAddress onto the invitation — so deleting the user does not erase the invitation's source-of-record copy.

4. metadata can hold recruiter-supplied custom PII: validator src/lib/validators/recruiting-invitations.ts uses z.record(z.string(), z.string()) for metadata, rejecting only keys with the reserved "_sys." prefix — arbitrary custom fields (e.g. phone, address) are permitted.

5. No admin path deletes/anonymizes a redeemed invitation:
   - DELETE route .../recruiting-invitations/[invitationId]/route.ts:186-188 rejects any non-pending invitation with cannotDeleteNonPending.
   - PATCH route 92-93 makes redeemed invitations immutable (invalidStatusTransition).
   - deleteRecruitingInvitation is invoked only behind the status!=="pending" guard.

6. The ONLY removal mechanism is the time-based sweep pruneRecruitingInvitations (data-retention-maintenance.ts:47-61), keyed on lt(recruitingInvitations.updatedAt, cutoff). Since updatedAt is bumped on any update (e.g. PATCH/metadata edit, account-password reset, failed-redeem counter increment in assignments/recruiting-invitations.ts:104-106), re-touching the row indefinitely postpones the cutoff, and there is no synchronous PII deletion tied to the erasure request.

CONCLUSION: Fulfilling a GDPR erasure request via "permanently delete user" leaves candidateName/candidateEmail/ipAddress/metadata intact on the recruiting_invitations row, with no synchronous admin mechanism to erase it. The finding is real (isReal=true).

title: Stale-claim reclaim leaks the original worker's active_tasks counter (permanent capacity loss)
scenario: buildClaimSql reclaims any submission in status 'queued'/'judging' whose judge_claimed_at is older than staleClaimTimeoutMs, regardless of whether the previous owner worker is still alive. The worker_bump CTE only increments active_tasks for the NEW claiming worker (@workerId); it never decrements the PREVIOUS owner's active_tasks. The only path that decrements a worker's counter is poll/route.ts (final verdict, guarded by the claim token) and the staleness sweep (which only fires when the worker stops heartbeating). Concrete failure: a worker hangs on a single submission (e.g. a runaway compile/exec it cannot kill, or a stuck Docker/crun container) but its heartbeat thread keeps reporting 'online'. After staleClaimTimeoutMs another worker reclaims that submission. The original worker's active_tasks stays incremented forever because (a) it never finalizes that submission with a matching claim token, and (b) it is still heartbeating so the sweep never reaps it or zeroes its counter. Each such event permanently reduces the worker's effective capacity (claim CTE gate 'active_tasks < concurrency'); after enough events the worker reaches active_tasks >= concurrency and silently stops accepting any work even though it is healthy, throttling the whole judge fleet during a contest/exam. The admin PATCH endpoint cannot fix it (it only edits alias); the only remedy is force-DELETE of the worker.
suggestedFix: When reclaiming a stale submission whose judge_worker_id is set and differs from the claiming worker, decrement that previous owner's active_tasks atomically in the same statement. Capture candidate.previous_worker_id (s.judge_worker_id before the UPDATE) and add a CTE: UPDATE judge_workers SET active_tasks = GREATEST(active_tasks - 1, 0) WHERE id = (SELECT previous_worker_id FROM candidate) AND previous_worker_id IS DISTINCT FROM @workerId. Alternatively, periodically reconcile each worker's active_tasks against the actual count of submissions in ('queued','judging') it currently owns.

No other path reconciles the previous owner's counter in the heartbeating-but-hung scenario:

1) poll/route.ts:168-175 decrements submission.judgeWorkerId, but after reclaim that column points to the NEW owner (B), so B's bump is reconciled while the original owner A's increment for that submission is orphaned. A's own later poll is token-fenced at line 154 (judge_claim_token must equal A's old token, which B overwrote) and rolls back via the rowCount===0 throw at 157-159, so A's late poll never decrements.

2) The staleness sweep (worker-staleness-sweep.ts:60-69) zeroes active_tasks ONLY on the stale->offline transition, gated by lastHeartbeatAt < computeActiveTasksResetCutoff (worker-staleness.ts:57-60). A worker that keeps heartbeating never goes stale, so the sweep never fires for it. The module/threshold comments (worker-staleness.ts:12-19) explicitly scope this mitigation to crashed/silent workers, not alive-but-hung ones.

3) heartbeat/route.ts:39-42 deliberately does NOT persist the worker's self-reported activeTasks (accepted as telemetry only, schema line 17), so even a healthy worker cannot correct the DB counter via heartbeat.

4) The problem-not-found reset (claim/route.ts:280-284) decrements only the current workerId and is token-guarded, so it cannot fix a submission reclaimed elsewhere.

5) Admin PATCH (admin/workers/[id]/route.ts:13-15,33-40) only edits alias; the sole remedy is DELETE (lines 65-127), which removes the worker row entirely (force-removal). The non-negative CHECK (schema.pg.ts:448) and GREATEST(...-1,0) floor (poll/route.ts:172) prevent underflow but never pull a leaked positive value back down for a live worker.

Leak source: buildClaimSql (src/lib/judge/claim-query.ts:45-47) reclaims any 'queued'/'judging' submission whose judge_claimed_at is older than staleClaimTimeoutMs, with no check on whether the previous owner is alive. - deregister/route.ts:59-67 (activeTasks:0): only on voluntary deregister, which a hung worker won't issue.

Because the claim gate is active_tasks < concurrency (claim-query.ts:35), after enough such events the healthy worker hits active_tasks >= concurrency, the worker_slot CTE returns empty, claim returns null / workerAtCapacity (claim/route.ts:214-216), and the worker silently stops accepting work. The only remedy is admin force-DELETE (admin/workers/[id]/route.ts:65-126). Scenario is genuinely reachable and exploitable in current code.

THE CODEBASE'S EXISTING A11Y DESIGN MAKES IT WORSE, NOT BETTER: code-surface.tsx:187-208 deliberately adds an Escape binding that blurs CodeMirror (view.contentDOM.blur()) to satisfy WCAG 2.1.2 "No Keyboard Trap" so Tab traverses the page normally. The comment even acknowledges "In fullscreen, the overlay's own Escape handler additionally exits fullscreen." This confirms there is intentionally NO focus trap: once the user blurs/Tabs out of the editor while in fullscreen, focus enters the normal page tab order, landing on the visually-obscured background controls (language selector at problem-submission-form.tsx:361-371, submit button, nav) that sit behind the z-50 overlay — exactly the claimed off-screen focus loss.

The only RAW_TEXTAREA_LANGUAGES entry is "whitespace" (language-map.ts:56); both the textarea branch and the CodeSurface branch render inside the same unmanaged overlay div, so neither branch mitigates the issue.

Conclusion: the finding is genuinely present and manifest right now. The fullscreen overlay is a de-facto modal with no role/aria-modal, no focus move-in, no focus trap, no background inert, and no focus restore on exit, violating WCAG 2.4.3 and 4.1.2 (and partially 1.3.1).

The missing dialog semantics, missing focus move-in, missing inert background, and missing focus restore are all present and manifest regardless. Core WCAG 2.4.3 and 4.1.2 issues are real and not upstream-mitigated.

title: Side-by-side diff distinguishes added/removed output lines by background color only (no text/symbol cue)
scenario: On a Wrong Answer verdict, students/candidates inspect expected-vs-actual output. In the side-by-side tab (SideBySideDiffView), differing lines are conveyed solely with a red (bg-red-50/dark:bg-red-950/30) or green (bg-green-50/dark:bg-green-950/30) row background — there is no '+'/'-' marker, no text label, and no ARIA. The unified view (UnifiedDiffView, lines 58-60) correctly adds a +/- symbol column, but the side-by-side view does not. Color-blind users (and the green/red backgrounds in dark mode are very low-saturation) and screen-reader users cannot tell which lines differ, so they cannot determine why their submission failed. This violates WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color).
suggestedFix: In SideBySideDiffView add a non-color indicator per differing row mirroring the unified view: a leading '-'/'+' (or '≠') marker cell for kind==="remove"/"add", and/or a visually-hidden text label (e.g. expected/missing line). This keeps the row-tint as an enhancement rather than the sole signal.
verifierReasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL. The cited code matches exactly. In SideBySideDiffView, the left panel (output-diff-view.tsx:83-99) and right panel (110-124) mark differing lines using only row background color: bg-red-50/dark:bg-red-950/30 for remove and bg-green-50/dark:bg-green-950/30 for add (lines 86-90, 113-117). Each side-by-side row has only two <td> cells — a line number (93-95, 120-122) and content (96, 123) — with NO +/- symbol column, unlike the unified view which adds one at line 58-60. Verified via grep: no sr-only, no aria-*, no role=, no title=, no data-*, and no CSS ::before/content marker (globals.css:139 is only a universal transition reset). Total 8 <td> = 4 unified (2 line-no + symbol + content) + 4 side-by-side (2 panels x line-no + content), confirming the side-by-side tables lack any non-color cue.

The path is reachable and manifest right now: submission-result-panel.tsx:103-110 renders <OutputDiffView> for every result.status === "wrong_answer" row — precisely the expected-vs-actual inspection scenario described.

I considered the strongest possible mitigation: the panels carry positional labels "Expected Output" (line 77) and "Actual Output" (line 104) plus per-line numbers, which could serve as a non-color cue. But this does NOT cover the failure case. toSideBySide (diff.ts:98-121) pairs consecutive remove/add lines onto the SAME row, placing remove content on the left and add content on the right simultaneously. Such a changed row has content + line number on both sides, making it visually identical to an unchanged "equal" row (which also has content on both sides) — the ONLY differentiator is the red/green background. Therefore colorblind users and screen-reader users cannot determine which lines differ. This is a genuine WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color) violation with no existing mitigation.

CONFIRMED REAL. Traced end-to-end: submission-result-panel.tsx:103-112 renders <OutputDiffView> for any result with status "wrong_answer" (expectedOutput/actualOutput non-null). OutputDiffView (output-diff-view.tsx:13-34) exposes two tabs; the "sideBySide" tab renders SideBySideDiffView. No auth/scope/validation guard blocks reaching this view — it is the standard Wrong Answer inspection UI and the tab is freely selectable.

In SideBySideDiffView (output-diff-view.tsx:70-133), the left-panel <tr> (lines 83-99) distinguishes diff lines ONLY via background color class: "bg-red-50 dark:bg-red-950/30" (remove) or "bg-green-50 dark:bg-green-950/30" (add) at lines 86-90. The right-panel <tr> (lines 110-124) does the same (green/red) at lines 113-117. The only <td> cells rendered are the line number (93-95, 120-122) and raw content (96, 123). There is NO +/- symbol column, NO text label, NO aria-label, NO <ins>/<del> semantic element, and NO visually-hidden screen-reader text. So changed vs. unchanged rows are indistinguishable to color-blind and screen-reader users.

Contrast with the sibling UnifiedDiffView (lines 36-68), which DOES provide a non-color cue: a dedicated symbol <td> at lines 58-60 renders "+", "-", or " " per line.kind. This proves the omission in the side-by-side view is a genuine inconsistency, not an accessible-by-design choice.

The underlying data supports a fix but is unused for it: SideBySidePair.left/right.kind ("equal"|"add"|"remove") is defined in diff.ts:18-21 and populated by toSideBySide (lines 94-127), yet output-diff-view.tsx reads kind only to pick the background color (86-90, 113-117), never to emit a symbol/label/ARIA.

This is a genuine WCAG 1.4.1 (Use of Color) failure that is currently manifest and reachable in the running app, with no upstream mitigation.

title: yellow-600 text on muted/light backgrounds fails 4.5:1 contrast (stderr label, timed-out marker)
